title: Roots in Austin
dek: Geeta Persad grew up in Austin, Texas — exploring the Texas Memorial Museum and auditing lectures at UT Austin as a child.
narrative: Long before she would return as a faculty member, Geeta Persad's intellectual curiosity was shaped by Austin itself. Her family has deep roots in the city, and she spent her childhood in the spaces that would later become her professional home.

Her family originates from Trinidad and Tobago — a small island nation whose economy has been both fueled and made fragile by petrochemical resources. That connection gave Persad an early, visceral awareness of the tension between energy needs and climate realities, a tension that would define her scientific career.
mapCaption: Grew up in Austin, Texas — with a family connection to UT Austin from childhood.

title: Geophysics at Stanford
dek: B.S. in Geophysics from Stanford University (2010) — the first step away from Austin and toward a career at the intersection of Earth science and climate.
narrative: Persad left Austin for Stanford University, where she earned a Bachelor of Science in Geophysics in 2010. The choice of geophysics reflected an early instinct to understand the physical systems underlying climate — not just the policy or the politics, but the mechanics of the Earth itself.

Stanford's environment also introduced her to the broader ecosystem of climate science and environmental policy, planting seeds for the interdisciplinary approach that would define her graduate work and beyond.

title: Physical Scientist at NOAA
dek: Before her doctorate, Persad served as a Physical Scientist at the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration — an early grounding in applied climate science.
narrative: Between her Stanford undergraduate years and enrolling at Princeton, Persad worked as a Physical Scientist at NOAA. This early professional experience in a federal science agency gave her direct exposure to how climate data is produced, used, and communicated to decision-makers.
The NOAA chapter established a practical dimension to her scientific formation — one that would later inform her commitment to making climate research legible and actionable for policymakers.
mapCaption: Physical Scientist at NOAA before enrolling at Princeton.

title: Doctoral formation at Princeton
dek: Ph.D. in Atmospheric and Oceanic Sciences from Princeton University (2016), with an NSF Graduate Research Fellowship and a certificate in Science, Technology, and Environmental Policy.
narrative: Persad chose Princeton in part because of the HMEI-STEP program — a fellowship that let her pursue rigorous scientific research while simultaneously studying its policy implications. Her dissertation focused on atmospheric aerosols: particle air pollutants produced by transportation, fossil fuel combustion, and agricultural burning, and how they impact the climate system.

A key project examined how international trade had shifted the location of black carbon aerosol emissions — particularly China's emissions driven by trade with other nations. She also attended the UN Rio+20 Conference in Brazil through the Princeton Energy and Climate Scholars Group, an experience that deepened her confidence in interdisciplinary spaces.

Her adviser Michael Oppenheimer, former chief scientist at the Environmental Defense Fund, became a formative mentor. Persad won consecutive Outstanding Student Paper Awards from the American Geophysical Union during this period for her work on aerosol-climate interactions.
proofPoints: NSF Graduate Research Fellow and Ford Science, Technology, and Environmental Policy Fellow.
Consecutive Outstanding Student Paper Awards from the American Geophysical Union.
Attended UN Rio+20 Conference in Brazil through Princeton Energy and Climate Scholars Group.
mapCaption: Ph.D. in Atmospheric and Oceanic Sciences, Princeton University, 2016.

evidenceProfile.strength: Multiple institutional sources plus fellowship records

title: Bridging science and policy at Carnegie and UCS
dek: Joint position as Research Associate at the Carnegie Institution for Science (Stanford) and Senior Climate Scientist at the Union of Concerned Scientists — applying climate modeling to real-world water and policy questions.
narrative: After Princeton, Persad held a joint position that embodied her dual commitment to fundamental science and societal impact. At the Carnegie Institution for Science, she worked with Dr. Ken Caldeira using global climate models to understand how climates might change as emissions patterns vary across the world — mapping who feels the effects of climate and air quality changes and translating findings into policy-relevant metrics.

At the Union of Concerned Scientists, she led the Western Water and Climate Program, applying that climate modeling expertise to water resource questions in the Western U.S. — especially California's complex managed water infrastructure. She worked directly with government agencies, legislatures, and community stakeholders to improve climate planning in water management.
This period crystallized her conviction that asking scientific questions in slightly different ways can align research more closely with decisions that policymakers actually have to make.
proofPoints: Led the Western Water and Climate Program at the Union of Concerned Scientists.
Research Associate at Carnegie Institution for Science with Dr. Ken Caldeira.
Worked directly with government agencies and legislatures on Western U.S. water planning.
mapCaption: Joint position at Carnegie Institution for Science (Stanford) and Union of Concerned Scientists, 2016–2020.

evidenceProfile.strength: Multiple institutional profiles plus first-person Q&A

title: Coming home: Assistant Professor at UT Austin
dek: Assistant Professor of Climate Science at the Jackson School of Geosciences, UT Austin — returning to her hometown 14 years after leaving for Stanford.
narrative: In 2020, Persad joined the Jackson School of Geosciences at The University of Texas at Austin as an Assistant Professor of Climate Science — returning to the city where she grew up. 'Coming home was a really big draw,' she said. 'I have a long family and Austinite connection to the university.'

She leads the Persad Aero-Climate Group, whose research centers on the role of atmospheric aerosol emissions in driving inter-regional differences in climate change and the emergence of heat and hydroclimate extremes. A growing branch of the lab's work applies this understanding to questions at the interface of climate and hydrology, with a focus on water management stress in the Central and Western U.S.

Persad was a critical driver in developing the new climate system science major in the Department of Earth and Planetary Sciences, and is passionate about building a diverse geoscience enterprise through mentorship and outreach. In 2021, the Environmental Defense Fund named her one of eight 'Generation Climate' leaders.
proofPoints: 2021: Named EDF 'Generation Climate' leader — one of eight honorees on the cover of EDF Solutions magazine.
2023: NOAA grant to study how changing aerosol pollution could influence near-term U.S. climate change.
2025: NSF CAREER Award for research on aerosol-driven compound climate risks.
mapCaption: Assistant Professor, Jackson School of Geosciences, UT Austin — returning to her hometown.

evidenceProfile.strength: Multiple UT Austin institutional sources plus awards trail
evidenceProfile.artifact: Faculty chapter — ready for full treatment

title: Aerosols, climate risk, and the science of compound extremes
dek: Persad's research group investigates how aerosol pollution drives spatially complex climate risks — and how rapidly evolving emissions represent a dangerous omission from near-term climate risk assessments.
narrative: The Persad Aero-Climate Group uses numerical global climate models to understand how atmospheric aerosol particles — the primary radiative forcing offset to greenhouse gases over the industrial era — drive inter-regional differences in the magnitude and rate of climate change.

A key insight driving the lab's current work: aerosol emissions are expected to change rapidly over the coming decades, driving strong, spatially complex trends in temperature, hydroclimate, and extreme events both near and far from emission sources. This makes them a dangerous omission from near-term climate risk assessments.

With her 2025 NSF CAREER Award, Persad is investigating how aerosol pollutants that produce smog contribute to compound climate risks — extreme temperature, rainfall, and humidity occurring simultaneously. The education component of the award will develop a computational field trip with the Texas Advanced Computing Center, giving undergraduates hands-on experience with Earth system model data.
proofPoints: 2023 IOP Science paper: 'Rapidly evolving aerosol emissions are a dangerous omission from near-term climate risk assessments.'
2023 NOAA grant for near-term aerosol climate risk research.
2025 NSF CAREER Award for compound aerosol-climate risk research.
mapCaption: Research spans global aerosol transport, monsoon systems, and Western U.S. water impacts.
